title: Looks and Books

jokes.text: Opening cold open: a boy approaches a girl and asks if she'll have sex with him in exchange for a joint, then his friend says 'I can't believe you did it.'
jokes.setup: A nervous kid is building to asking something. The friend's reaction 'I can't believe you did it' frames it as a dare.
jokes.punchline: What he actually asked — offering a joint for sex — is so brazen and absurd for a high school hallway that the audacity is the joke. The friend's stunned admiration makes it worse/better.
jokes.explanation: Strong cold open. Works because it drops us into the world with no setup — the audacity of the ask is funny, the friend's reverent reaction sells it. Economy is excellent. Not character-specific enough to score higher.

jokes.text: Nick describes their upcoming gig as being played in a 'most rock-tagious way.'
jokes.setup: Nick is excitedly pitching their gig, listing real professional equipment names to sound legit.
jokes.punchline: 'Rock-tagious' — a made-up portmanteau of 'rock' and 'contagious' delivered with total sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Classic Nick Andopolis move — he's so earnest about his drumming dreams that he invents his own rock vocabulary. 'Rock-tagious' is perfectly character-specific. The contrast between the legitimate gear references and the made-up word is well-structured.

jokes.text: Lindsay explains she can't lend the station wagon because her dad is so attached he named it 'Betty.'
jokes.setup: Daniel is pressuring Lindsay to take her parents' car. She resists, saying they're 'insane' about it.
jokes.punchline: The specific, specific detail that her dad calls the car 'Betty' — which immediately explains everything and nothing.
jokes.explanation: Harold Weir naming his station wagon 'Betty' is a perfectly calibrated suburban-dad detail. It's funny because it's so specific and so believable, and it makes the subsequent car crash land harder emotionally.

jokes.text: Daniel: 'Just run over the stupid squirrel, Linze.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay swerved to avoid a squirrel, causing chaos in the car. Everyone is rattled.
jokes.punchline: Daniel's completely unbothered advice: just kill the squirrel. Delivered as obvious common sense.
jokes.explanation: Pure Daniel Desario nihilism in four words. Maximum economy, perfect character voice. His complete indifference to the squirrel while chaos erupts around him is very funny.

jokes.text: Kim and Daniel argue over directions — 'It's on Hancock.' 'It's on Warren.' 'I was just there.' — while simultaneously Nick defends Joe Jackson's bass player and Kim spots Valerie's house.
jokes.setup: Everyone in the car is talking over each other with competing agendas.
jokes.punchline: The cacophony of wrong directions, music opinions, and neighborhood sightseeing perfectly captures why this car ride is doomed. The comedy is cumulative chaos.
jokes.explanation: Great ensemble cringe-escalation. Each character speaks in their own isolated lane simultaneously, which is naturalistic and funny. Nick's sincere defense of Joe Jackson's bass player amid the chaos is a particularly good character note.

jokes.text: Ken announces 'I got a big one a-brewing' and threatens a fart in the enclosed car.
jokes.setup: The car is already chaotic with arguments about directions and music.
jokes.punchline: Ken's cheerful announcement of an imminent fart — 'IT'S COMING!' — as if reporting weather — adds biological warfare to the existing chaos.
jokes.explanation: Classic Ken — deadpan delivery of juvenile chaos. 'A big one a-brewing' is slightly elevated language for a fart announcement, which is the joke. Not the sharpest material but fits Ken's character and escalates the car disaster well.

jokes.text: The car crash — Lindsay, distracted by the fart chaos and Ken pointing out the house, hits a parked car.
jokes.setup: The car has been a chaos bomb for two minutes — wrong directions, farts, music arguments, everyone yelling.
jokes.punchline: The inevitable crash. What makes it funny rather than just dramatic is that the fart was the final distraction that caused it — biological warfare defeating a station wagon called Betty.
jokes.explanation: A textbook escalation payoff. The entire car-ride sequence has been building to this — every element (wrong directions, farts, Ken yelling) contributed to the crash. The structure is excellent. It earns its disaster fully.

jokes.text: Sam: 'I hate being in school after it's out. Makes me feel like a janitor.' Bill: 'Janitors are cool. I'd like to be a janitor.'
jokes.setup: Sam complains about being in school after hours with a colorful but relatable comparison.
jokes.punchline: Bill immediately and sincerely counters that janitors are cool and he'd like to be one — without irony.
jokes.explanation: Classic Bill Haverchuck. Sam's janitor line is good but Bill's sincere counter is what elevates it. Bill is the show's secret weapon — his literal, un-ironic responses to normal social cues are deeply funny. The follow-up where Bill defends janitor salary ('makes up for all that gross stuff') makes it even better. This is character comedy at its best — only Bill would say this.

jokes.text: Bill defends janitor pay: 'Janitors make way more than teachers. It makes up for all that gross stuff they have to do.'
jokes.setup: Neal mocks the janitor comparison with a reference to cleaning up vomit.
jokes.punchline: Bill has clearly thought this through economically. His janitor advocacy is genuine and researched.

jokes.text: The geeks debate why Cindy likes a guy with feathered hair. Neal: 'That's women. They get turned on by weird stuff.'
jokes.setup: Sam is baffled that Cindy seems attracted to a guy just because of his hair. Neal and Bill explain women to him.
jokes.punchline: Neal's authoritative pronouncement — 'That's women. They get turned on by weird stuff' — delivered with total confidence by a kid who has clearly no more idea than Sam.
jokes.explanation: Neal as the self-appointed authority on women is a running geek-comedy trope but it works here because of how confidently wrong he is. The specificity of 'weird stuff' — vague but delivered as settled wisdom — is good.

jokes.text: Bill: 'All the men my mom dates have feathered hair. I heard her talking to her girlfriends. She said any guy with feathered hair is foxy.'
jokes.setup: The geeks are theorizing about what makes feathered hair attractive.
jokes.punchline: Bill casually reveals his mother's romantic life and preferences while trying to be helpful, with zero awareness of how much he's sharing.
jokes.explanation: This is a great Bill moment. He's genuinely trying to contribute useful data, sourced from eavesdropping on his single mother's social life. 'She said any guy with feathered hair is foxy' — the word 'foxy' is perfect, and the overshare is done with complete innocence. The dark undertone of a kid learning about romance from his dating mom adds texture. High character integration — only Bill delivers this line this way.

jokes.text: Kim to the angry car owner: 'Hey, lady. There's something you might not have heard of. It's called insurance.'
jokes.setup: The car owner is furious and threatening to make Lindsay pay for every cent of damage.
jokes.punchline: Kim's intervention — condescending, legally naive, and completely unhelpful — delivered as if she's dropping wisdom.
jokes.explanation: Very Kim. She steps in to 'help' in a way that makes everything worse. The phrase 'something you might not have heard of' as if she's introducing the concept of insurance to this adult woman is the perfect Kim register.

jokes.text: Kim, after being told to stay away: 'Yeah. Who wants to be near you anyways. You're too sexy.'
jokes.setup: The car owner tells Kim to stay away from her.
jokes.punchline: Kim's comeback — 'You're too sexy' — is so bizarrely off-script as an insult-retort that it lands as pure Kim chaos energy.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's sharpest line. Kim, in the middle of a disaster she helped cause, tells the victim she's 'too sexy.' It's absurdist, surprising, and perfectly character-specific — Kim's defenses are so scrambled that this is what comes out. It couldn't be said by any other character. The economy is perfect: three words that tell you everything about Kim. This comfortably reaches 8+ territory.

jokes.text: Millie, trying to comfort a distressed Lindsay after the car crash: 'If you want, I can go get my Uno deck.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is emotionally devastated — car crash, parental fury, potential police involvement. Millie checks in.
jokes.punchline: Millie's solution to existential teenage crisis: Uno.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Millie means this 100% sincerely — Uno is her solution, her comfort, her world. The gap between Lindsay's actual catastrophe and Millie's offered remedy is devastating and sweet and funny all at once. Perfect economy, perfect character integration. Only Millie Kentner says this. The callback later in the episode ('Can we still play Uno sometimes?') retroactively deepens this moment.

jokes.text: Millie tells an elaborate story about a mathlete getting a bloody nose, sneezing blood everywhere, and fainting — then walks it back: 'Well, no, but she was gonna. It was pretty funny.'
jokes.setup: Millie is trying to cheer Lindsay up with a funny story from the mathletes scrimmage.
jokes.punchline: After a vivid, detailed buildup including fainting, she admits the fainting didn't actually happen — 'she was gonna.' The anti-climax is the joke, and Millie still insists 'it was pretty funny.'
jokes.explanation: Excellent structural joke. The setup is detailed and escalating (bloody nose → sneeze → blood everywhere → sees it on shirt → faints), then the rug pull: the best part didn't happen. Millie's confident 'it was pretty funny' after undermining her own story is a great character note — she's so earnest she can't see why this is less impressive now. Strong structure score.

jokes.text: Sam tries to put on a backpack while worrying: 'How am I gonna get this on without messing up my hair?' — followed by an audible RIP.
jokes.setup: Sam has apparently attempted a new hairstyle to impress Cindy. He's putting his backpack on.
jokes.punchline: The sound of something ripping — either his shirt or his carefully constructed hair situation — right after his worried question. The audio cue does all the work.
jokes.explanation: Great economy — the setup question and then the immediate [RIP] is perfect timing. Sam's vanity being punished instantly is satisfying comedy. The visual joke (his hair disaster) is inferred but clear.

jokes.text: After Sam appears in new clothes, a neighbor says 'Don't the Weirs look nice today?' and both Weir kids say 'Thank you' — then Sam's being mocked at school: 'What, did you just come from church?'
jokes.setup: Sam has dressed up to impress Cindy. His parents and neighbors are complimentary.
jokes.punchline: The contrast: what reads as 'nice' to adults reads as 'church clothes' to peers. The gap between parental approval and teenage cool is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition is the joke — adults love it, peers mock it. The 'church' read is funny because it's probably accurate. Well-structured irony through scene juxtaposition.

jokes.text: Lindsay uses sarcasm on Daniel: 'No. Not at all. He was really happy that I stole his car and smashed it.'
jokes.setup: Daniel casually asks if her dad was 'pissed' about the car.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's deadpan sarcastic response, delivered flatly, to Daniel's obvious question.
jokes.explanation: Functional sarcasm. Lindsay's deadpan delivery is good but the joke form is predictable. The 'smashed it' specificity is the only lift.

jokes.text: After Lindsay explodes at the freaks, Daniel says: 'Maybe you're just on your period.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is genuinely and righteously furious, making valid points about being used.
jokes.punchline: Daniel's reductive dismissal — the classic 'on your period' dodge — which Lindsay then weaponizes back at him.
jokes.explanation: Not a clever joke on its own but the setup for Lindsay's brilliant comeback (id 23). Daniel's lazy dismissal is accurate to his character and sets up what follows.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Yeah, Daniel, that's exactly it. I'm on my period. You figured it out.' / Daniel: 'It was a joke.' / Lindsay: 'Oh, sorry. It's hard to pick up on the subtlety of your wit.'
jokes.setup: Daniel made a dismissive 'period' joke. Lindsay has a choice — get mad or destroy him.
jokes.punchline: She chooses sardonic agreement then the kill shot: 'It's hard to pick up on the subtlety of your wit.' — treating Daniel's witlessness as something that requires subtlety to detect.
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's best moment in the episode. The structure is perfect: false agreement (feeds his ego), then the twist that the agreement itself is the insult. 'The subtlety of your wit' is the killer — it implies Daniel has no wit by ironically attributing subtlety to it. This is genuine comic intelligence from both the character and the writers. Economy is excellent. Reaches 8+ because the construction is genuinely elegant.

jokes.text: After Lindsay storms off, Daniel: 'What was that all about?' Ken: 'I don't know, but it was pretty funny.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay just scorched everyone and walked away.
jokes.punchline: Ken's completely detached assessment — 'pretty funny' — as if he watched a stranger's argument rather than participated.
jokes.explanation: Very Ken Miller. His total emotional detachment — finding Lindsay's pain 'pretty funny' — is both a laugh and a character reveal. The economy is nearly perfect. 'I don't know, but it was pretty funny' does a lot in seven words.

jokes.text: Kim: 'It's like hanging out with my grandma.' (about Lindsay)
jokes.setup: The freaks are debriefing Lindsay's departure and deciding she was always annoying.
jokes.punchline: Kim's specific comparison — grandma — is funny because it implies Kim has a very particular grandmother who is a drag.
jokes.explanation: A solid character line. The specificity of 'grandma' over a generic 'boring person' comparison is the lift. Kim's contempt is consistent.

jokes.text: Ken's plan: 'I'm gonna be, like, a lawyer or something. I'm gonna put the pole on trial. I'm gonna get guys out of jail and stuff.'
jokes.setup: The group is now forced to articulate their actual plans for life.
jokes.punchline: 'Put the pole on trial' — presumably 'the police' — and 'get guys out of jail and stuff.' Ken's lawyer fantasy is simultaneously streetwise and profoundly vague.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Ken Miller. The malapropism 'put the pole on trial' (the police) combined with the vague 'stuff' at the end is perfect for Ken — he has just enough civic awareness to want to fight the system but zero specificity. His legal theory is entirely vibes-based. Very funny and very character-specific.

jokes.text: Kim: 'Yeah. Maybe you can break Daniel out of there.' / Daniel: 'Screw you, man. I'm not going to jail.'
jokes.setup: Ken has just pitched his defense-lawyer plan.
jokes.punchline: Kim assumes Daniel will need this service. Daniel's protest — 'I'm not going to jail' — is exactly what someone going to jail would say.
jokes.explanation: Quick double-punch. Kim's assumption is the setup, Daniel's protest-too-much is the punchline. It implies a shared understanding of Daniel's probable future. Efficient.

jokes.text: Nick, earnestly: 'You guys, I'm telling you, this is not about the accident. Lindsay's really depressed that I broke up with her. She's got to get over it though, you know?'
jokes.setup: The group is analyzing Lindsay's blowup. Lindsay clearly ended things with Nick, not the other way around.
jokes.punchline: Nick has completely rewritten the breakup in his head — he broke up with her — and delivers this delusion with genuine compassion for her.
jokes.explanation: Nick's delusional self-narrative is one of the show's running jokes with real comedic depth. He's not lying — he genuinely believes this. The 'she's got to get over it though' is devastating in its misplaced magnanimity. Only Nick Andopolis has this particular brand of oblivious self-regard. High character integration.

jokes.text: Nick reveals his life plans: 'I'm gonna be a deejay, man.' [Beat] 'And maybe, um, a lumberjack.'
jokes.setup: The group is going around declaring their plans. Nick has been critiquing everyone else.
jokes.punchline: DJ is the expected Nick answer. 'And maybe, um, a lumberjack' — after a pause, as if he just thought of it — is the unexpected escalation. These two careers have essentially no overlap.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The structure is perfect: DJ is credible within Nick's character (just barely), then the pause and 'a lumberjack' is the non-sequitur that makes it great. The 'um' is crucial — it sounds improvised, like even Nick doesn't know where this came from. The juxtaposition of indoor-electronic entertainment and outdoor manual labor, presented with equal casual conviction, is genuinely funny writing. Economy is near-perfect: 11 words including the 'um.'

jokes.text: The Texas Instruments joke: 'The name of the company is Texas Instruments, but I heard it's made in Taiwan.' [All laugh]
jokes.setup: The mathletes are hanging out, talking about calculators.
jokes.punchline: A mild anti-corporate observation that's apparently the funniest thing any of them have heard. The joke is that THEY find it hilarious — it tells us everything about mathlete social dynamics.
jokes.explanation: The joke within the joke: this isn't very funny, but the mathletes love it, which establishes the social world Lindsay is re-entering. The real laugh is at the mathletes' reaction, not the line itself. Smart meta-comedy.

jokes.text: Lindsay's dad won't buy her a calculator: 'He says Einstein did fine without one and so should I.'
jokes.setup: The mathletes are discussing their calculators. Lindsay doesn't have one.
jokes.punchline: Harold Weir's Einstein logic — using a genius who predated calculators to justify not buying his daughter a $20 calculator.
jokes.explanation: Excellent Harold Weir characterization in absentia. 'Einstein did fine without one' is perfect dad-logic — technically true, entirely inapplicable. The character integration is high because this is so specifically Harold: cheap, principled-sounding, accidentally insulting.

jokes.text: Millie: 'I think he had a crush on you.' [about the 40-year-old judge at intra-districts] / Everyone: 'EW, he was like 40 years old. That's so gross.'
jokes.setup: They're reminiscing about a judge who called Lindsay 'the human calculator.'
jokes.punchline: Millie floats the crush theory with complete sincerity. The group's horrified reaction to a 40-year-old judge having a crush is appropriate — but Millie apparently considered it a flattering possibility.
jokes.explanation: Millie being the one to identify and offer a creepy adult crush as a compliment is perfectly her — she's so sheltered and earnest that it doesn't register as wrong. Good character moment.

jokes.text: Gordie defends his weight: 'My whole family's big-boned. It's genetic. Besides, the world loves jolly fat guys. Burl Ives, Jackie Gleason... Santa Claus, uh, Curly, Raymond Burr.' Sam/Neal: 'No. Raymond Burr's not jolly.' Gordon: 'Well, he was extremely nice to me at last year's auto show.'
jokes.setup: Bill's comment that 'the world loves jolly fat guys' leads Gordon to list examples.
jokes.punchline: The list escalates beautifully: Burl Ives (jolly), Jackie Gleason (debatable), Santa Claus (fine), Curly (OK), Raymond Burr (Ironside — decidedly not jolly) — and then Gordon's defense of Raymond Burr's personal warmth based on a meet-and-greet at an auto show.
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke. The escalating list of 'jolly fat guys' is funny throughout, but including Raymond Burr — Perry Mason, the stern, intense TV lawyer — as a jolly fat guy breaks the pattern perfectly. The punchline 'well, he was extremely nice to me at last year's auto show' is exceptional: Gordon has a personal relationship with Raymond Burr's warmth, from a single meeting at an auto show, and he uses this to defend the categorization. The specificity (last year's auto show, not some generic encounter) is what elevates it. Highly quotable, original, and specific to this character in this world. This is what an 8+ looks like.

jokes.text: Cindy: 'Today in chemistry, I sat on a piece of chocolate and had to go home and change my pants.' Sam laughs too eagerly: 'OH. YEAH.'
jokes.setup: Sam has dressed up to impress Cindy. She stops to say hi and borrow a pencil.
jokes.punchline: Cindy volunteers a mildly embarrassing bathroom-adjacent story and Sam's response — 'OH. YEAH.' — is his desperate attempt to seem cool and relatable about an anecdote that required no specific response.
jokes.explanation: Great cringe moment. Cindy's story is slightly embarrassing but normal-teen. Sam's reaction — over-enthusiastic affirmation of her chocolate-pants incident — is the perfect illustration of his inability to be normal around Cindy. His 'OH. YEAH.' implies he's thrilled by any intimacy she shares, including clothing crises.

jokes.text: Cindy asks if Sam wore a hat 'because your hair looks kind of flat' — referring to his failed feathering attempt.
jokes.setup: Sam has been working on his feathered hair all episode to impress Cindy.
jokes.punchline: Cindy's observation that his hair looks 'flat' — the exact opposite of the intended effect — delivered with no awareness that she's crushing his effort.
jokes.explanation: The callback to Sam's hair project lands well. His entire morning effort — the backpack rip, the stress — produces 'flat hair' as Cindy's verdict. The irony is well-structured even if the individual joke isn't particularly sophisticated.

jokes.text: Sam: 'I dress OK.' / Bill: 'Sam, you look like your mother dresses you.' / Sam: 'Hey, well, at least I don't dress like a ventriloquist's dummy.'
jokes.setup: Bill is giving Sam fashion criticism.
jokes.punchline: Sam's comeback — 'ventriloquist's dummy' — aimed at Neal's overly formal/preppy style. The specificity of ventriloquist's dummy (as opposed to just 'fancy') is the comedy.
jokes.explanation: The exchange builds nicely but 'ventriloquist's dummy' for Neal's dress style is the gem — it's specific, visual, and unexpectedly precise as an insult. You immediately picture Neal in a tiny tuxedo on someone's knee. Good economy.

jokes.text: Neal: 'Change your clothes, change your life.'
jokes.setup: Neal has been earnestly advising Sam on his appearance.
jokes.punchline: The aphoristic sign-off — 'Change your clothes, change your life' — delivered with the conviction of a self-help guru.
jokes.explanation: Very quotable. Neal as a tiny confidence guru dispensing commercial-grade wisdom is funny. The economy is excellent. Becomes funnier when clothes do NOT change Sam's life.

jokes.text: Harold Weir complains about the body shop: 'We live right outside Detroit, and they can't find a fender? Now I get two weeks of driving around town looking like some kind of hillbilly.'
jokes.setup: Harold is venting about the auto repair situation with the damaged station wagon.
jokes.punchline: 'Looking like some kind of hillbilly' — the real injury isn't the car damage, it's the social indignity of driving a damaged car in Michigan car country.
jokes.explanation: Harold Weir's particular brand of practical outrage. The Detroit detail is good local color — he lives in auto-manufacturing country and can't get a fender. The 'hillbilly' concern reveals his social anxieties.

jokes.text: Jean Weir offers to take Sam to the mall with credit cards and suggests Magic Pan and crepes. Sam: 'Oh, no. Mom! You always get me something stupid, like Garanimals or something like that.'
jokes.setup: Sam wants money to buy his own clothes. Jean immediately pivots to a full mother-son mall outing.
jokes.punchline: Garanimals — the brand of color-coded kids' clothes designed to match automatically. Sam's fear that he'll come home with toddler-coordination clothes is specific and character-perfect.
jokes.explanation: Garanimals is the perfect period-specific brand reference. Jean's immediate escalation to credit cards + Magic Pan crepes is a funny encapsulation of her mothering style. Sam's horror is proportional. Good 1980 specificity.

jokes.text: Harold: 'A man has to learn to dress himself. You know, you got to cut those apron strings sometime.' [Said directly to Jean, who is the one with the apron strings he's referencing]
jokes.setup: Jean wants to take Sam shopping. Harold sides with Sam.
jokes.punchline: Harold tells Jean to cut the apron strings — to her face, somewhat obliviously — and she has to stand there and take it.
jokes.explanation: Harold doesn't realize (or does realize) that telling Jean to cut 'apron strings' while siding with Sam against her is itself a form of apron-string-cutting. The irony is mild but character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Then drink a cup of coffee and get it together, Daniel.'
jokes.setup: Daniel complains he went to all his classes the day before but could barely stay awake.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's no-nonsense prescription — coffee — delivered with complete impatience.
jokes.explanation: Good economy, appropriate to Lindsay's returning-achiever mode. The gap between Daniel's existential classroom struggle and Lindsay's solution (coffee) is the joke.

jokes.text: The Carl Sagan mathlete joke: '...And Carl Sagan says, That's not a cosine. You don't know your asymptote from a hole in the ground.' [Mathletes laugh uproariously]
jokes.setup: Lindsay arrives at a mathlete gathering. They're laughing at something.
jokes.punchline: The 'asymptote from a hole in the ground' is a math-nerd riff on 'can't tell your ass from a hole in the ground.' It's mild but delivered as if it's the funniest thing ever said. Again, the joke is partly the mathletes' reaction.
jokes.explanation: The writers get double value here: the wordplay is genuinely clever (asymptote / ass is a real pun), but it's framed so the joke is also the mathlete bubble where this passes for comedy gold. Meta-comedy through character.

jokes.text: Shelly: 'Oh. Was that Kim girl with you? She's pregnant, you know. My neighbor works at the free clinic, and he said that he saw her over there the other day.'
jokes.setup: Shelly is cataloging everything wrong with Lindsay's freaks friends.
jokes.punchline: Shelly has specific gossip — sourced from a neighbor's professional violation of HIPAA — delivered as fact.
jokes.explanation: The source of Shelly's gossip — 'my neighbor works at the free clinic' — is funnier than the gossip itself. Someone is violating medical privacy to gossip about a teenager and Shelly is treating it as journalism. Good Shelly character work.

jokes.text: Lindsay defends Kim: 'Just because a girl chooses to live her life differently than you do doesn't mean she's automatically barefoot and pregnant.' / Shelly: 'I didn't say she was barefoot.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay defends Kim with a passionate feminist retort using the idiom 'barefoot and pregnant.'
jokes.punchline: Shelly literally parses the idiom — 'I didn't say she was barefoot' — because she's both defensive and insufficiently familiar with the expression.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best constructed joke. Lindsay delivers an impassioned defense using a standard feminist idiom, and Shelly — too literal, too focused on defending herself — picks the one word she can challenge. 'I didn't say she was barefoot' implies she did say pregnant. The gap between Lindsay's rhetorical register and Shelly's pedantic counter-response is perfectly structured. It's also character-revealing for both: Lindsay's growing social conscience, Shelly's defensive literalism. Reaches 8+ comfortably.

jokes.text: Kowchevski: 'OK. You're right, Lindsay, the team needs you desperately. I mean, if it wasn't for Shelly, we'd be in the toilet.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is demanding her spot back on the team. Kowchevski finally admits the truth.
jokes.punchline: His capitulation is comically candid — 'if it wasn't for Shelly, we'd be in the toilet' said to Lindsay while Shelly is presumably nearby.
jokes.explanation: Kowchevski's coach-brain overriding his teacher-diplomacy is funny and honest. The 'in the toilet' bluntness is unexpectedly candid for a school official.

jokes.text: The clothing store scene: Sam walks in eating a hot dog. Store attendant: 'Hey, guy, you can't eat in here.' / Sam: 'I'm not done.' / Attendant: 'I can't throw it away.' / Attendant: 'Well, I can't have you getting mustard all over our garments.'
jokes.setup: Three geeks enter a trendy clothing store. Sam is mid-hot-dog.
jokes.punchline: The standoff — Sam cannot conceive of throwing away an unfinished hot dog, attendant cannot conceive of mustard on his garments — ends with Sam apparently eating it whole to resolve the situation.
jokes.explanation: Excellent geek-world-collision-with-cool comedy. The insistence on 'I'm not done' as a moral position about food waste is funny. 'Garments' is a great word choice by the attendant — it places him in aspirational retail. The visual of Sam cramming the hot dog works well.

jokes.text: Clothing store attendant: 'You wanna be a stud, or do you wanna be a super stud?' / Neal: 'Super stud, Sam. Go for super stud.'
jokes.setup: Sam says he wants to buy clothes that make him look like the best-dressed kid in school.
jokes.punchline: The binary — stud vs. super stud — as a sales taxonomy, and Neal's immediate coaching from the sidelines.
jokes.explanation: The salesman's binary is funny in a sleazy retail way — he's upselling confidence categories. Neal's instant coaching ('Go for super stud') is funnier — he has no authority here but can't help advising.

jokes.text: The salesman unveils 'a Parisian Nightsuit' and predicts 'every trendsetting guy in this city's gonna own one.'
jokes.setup: The salesman has taken them to 'the latest thing in Europe' with enormous fanfare.
jokes.punchline: The 'Parisian Nightsuit' is clearly a jumpsuit/leisure suit — something we'll see mocked as absurd once Sam wears it to school.
jokes.explanation: The 'Parisian Nightsuit' as a sales name for what is clearly a jumpsuit is funny. The setup is good — the salesman's credibility and confidence make it land harder when Sam wears it. Plant for a much bigger payoff.

jokes.text: The salesman's testimonial: 'You know, I'm not a handsome man, but I learned how to style my hair, started wearing some of these clothes. It's been pretty cool.'
jokes.setup: The salesman is trying to close the sale by sharing personal transformation.
jokes.punchline: His honest self-assessment — 'I'm not a handsome man' — followed by 'It's been pretty cool' as the conclusion of his Cinderella story.
jokes.explanation: The anti-inspirational testimonial. He doesn't claim to be transformed — he admits he wasn't handsome and remains presumably not handsome, but it's 'been pretty cool.' The understatement and honesty make it funnier than a normal sales pitch.

jokes.text: Kowchevski, during the Millie-Shelly conflict over lineup: 'This isn't the last chopper out of Saigon, so can we please just crank down the drama a notch, OK?'
jokes.setup: The mathletes are having a dramatic meltdown over who's on the team.
jokes.punchline: Kowchevski deploys a Vietnam War reference — 'last chopper out of Saigon' — to defuse a middle school math team conflict, which is a massive tonal mismatch.
jokes.explanation: Excellent teacher-character joke. The Saigon reference is absurdist because of the scale mismatch — he's treating a math team lineup dispute with the gravity of wartime evacuation, then inverting that by pointing out it ISN'T Saigon. The '1980 setting' makes this feel recent and slightly edgy. 'Crank down the drama a notch' is also a good line. This comfortably reaches 8.

jokes.text: Sam enters school in the Parisian Nightsuit to Joe Jackson's 'Look Sharp' — and students immediately start laughing and pointing.
jokes.setup: Sam bought the Parisian Nightsuit on the salesman's promise it would make him look like a trendsetter. The music cue 'Look Sharp' promises transformation.
jokes.punchline: The immediate, universal mockery. The song plays straight while everyone laughs — the irony of the song title against the reality is the comedic frame.

jokes.text: Jock: 'It's the new disco Ken Doll! Hey, pull the string and make him talk!'
jokes.setup: Sam has entered in the jumpsuit. Students are processing what they're seeing.
jokes.punchline: 'Pull the string and make him talk' — the Ken Doll comparison with the wind-up doll mechanic is precise mockery.
jokes.explanation: Good mockery. 'Disco Ken Doll' is a strong image. The 'pull the string' callback to the wind-up toy adds a second joke beat.

jokes.text: Neal and Bill apparently debating whether farts are flammable: 'I'm telling you, you can light them. It's methane.' / 'Yeah, but what if the flame goes back inside you? Do you — do you explode?'
jokes.setup: Sam has just entered in the jumpsuit and chaos is erupting. Neal and Bill are in their own world.
jokes.punchline: While Sam's humiliation unfolds, Neal and Bill are pursuing an independent scientific inquiry into combustible flatulence, with Bill genuinely worried about explosion.
jokes.explanation: A great background joke that works because of the dramatic irony — Sam is being humiliated and his best friends are absorbed in fart physics. Bill's sincere concern about internal combustion is his best line type. Also a callback to Ken's car fart in spirit.

jokes.text: A student: 'I guess Elvis hasn't left the building.'
jokes.setup: Sam enters in the jumpsuit — which apparently resembles an Elvis jumpsuit.
jokes.punchline: The Elvis reference is the correct read on the outfit and 'hasn't left the building' is a period-appropriate joke.
jokes.explanation: Functional joke, period-appropriate, but not a strong specific line. The setup does the work, not the punchline.

jokes.text: A student: 'No, no. It's a jumpsuit. My grandpa in Fort Lauderdale wears them all the time because he's too lazy to put on pants.'
jokes.setup: Sam is being mocked from multiple angles. Someone says it's a 'Parisian' outfit.
jokes.punchline: The grandpa-Fort Lauderdale-lazy-pants explanation is the most deflating possible context for what Sam thought was trendy European fashion.

jokes.text: Neal: 'Why did you buy that?' / Sam: 'Hey, you were the one that said I should dress better.' / Neal: 'Yeah, I didn't say you should dress like Evel Knievel.'
jokes.setup: Sam is fleeing through school, surrounded by friends trying to escort him out.
jokes.punchline: Neal's disavowal — 'I didn't say Evel Knievel' — is perfect because it both accepts minimal responsibility and provides the episode's best description of the outfit.

jokes.text: Bill, while escorting the fleeing Sam: 'I feel like I'm in the Secret Service.'
jokes.setup: The geeks are hustling Sam toward the exit, flanking him, trying to shield him from mockery.
jokes.punchline: Bill's complete absorption in the excitement of the moment — the Secret Service comparison — while missing that Sam is being humiliated.
jokes.explanation: Pure Bill Haverchuck. He is 100% sincere — this IS exciting for Bill. The Secret Service comparison reveals his imagination and total dissociation from social stakes. Only Bill in this moment says this. Economy is excellent. Reaches 8+ because of the perfect character integration and the delightful contrast with Sam's misery.

jokes.text: Sam tries to escape through the front door. Teacher stops him: 'Yeah? Unless you're going out to drive a bus, you'd better turn around and get your butt to class.'
jokes.setup: Sam tries to escape the school. He's approached by a teacher.
jokes.punchline: 'Unless you're going out to drive a bus' — the teacher's arbitrary specific exception to leaving school. Why a bus? Because it's the one vehicle that would make leaving school a legitimate school-affiliated activity.
jokes.explanation: Good teacher-logic joke. 'Drive a bus' is a funny specific exception that implies a whole bureaucratic rule system. The administrator isn't wrong — that might be the one thing that justifies leaving.

jokes.text: Teacher, as Sam leaves in defeat: 'Nice dud, Junior.'
jokes.setup: Sam has been turned back to class in his jumpsuit.
jokes.punchline: 'Nice dud' — 'dud' as both a failure and a garment ('dude'/'duds' as slang for clothes). Whether intentional wordplay or not, it lands perfectly.
jokes.explanation: 'Nice dud' is a compact gem — it works as 'nice dud' (failure) and 'nice duds' (nice clothes, sarcastically). Two meanings in two words. Good economy.

jokes.text: In English class, as Sam goes to the board in his jumpsuit: a jock coughs 'HOMO,' then another coughs 'HOMO,' then scattered coughing insults fill the room.
jokes.setup: Sam is called to the board in his jumpsuit in front of the whole class.
jokes.punchline: The coordinated cough-insult technique — a classic high school passive-aggressive bullying method — deployed with escalating commitment.
jokes.explanation: The cough-insult is period-authentic and the escalation (one, then another, then scattered) is well-staged. It's cringe-comedy through Sam's helplessness. Not the strongest material but serves the scene.

jokes.text: The English teacher's response to the coughing insults: 'Now if Sam wearing something different to express his individuality makes him a homo, well then, I guess we should all be proud to be homos.'
jokes.setup: The teacher has witnessed the classroom bullying.
jokes.punchline: The teacher's well-intentioned defense is more mortifying than the bullying — she's now announced to the whole class that Sam is to be proud of his 'homo'-ness and invited them to join. Sam visibly dies.
jokes.explanation: The cruelty of adult advocacy done wrong. The teacher means well but by earnestly repeating 'homo' and inviting the class to share the identity, she's made it worse. The joke is in her self-congratulatory delivery versus the catastrophic social outcome for Sam. Very good cringe structure.

jokes.text: Harris the D&D kid, evaluating Daniel: 'No. You're not a loser, 'cause you have sex. But if you weren't having sex, then we could definitely debate the issue.'
jokes.setup: Daniel has asked a geek in the library what he thinks of him — vulnerable, genuine, seeking an outside perspective.
jokes.punchline: Harris provides a completely logical framework for loser-hood with sex as the decisive variable. His 'we could definitely debate the issue' implies he'd enjoy that debate.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best pure joke. Harris has built a binary taxonomy of personhood — loser/not-loser — with sex as the singular distinguishing criterion, delivered as settled academic judgment. 'We could definitely debate the issue' is extraordinary — it implies he finds the question genuinely interesting and that his conclusion might be subject to revision. The honesty, the logic, and the total social unawareness make this a great character joke. Harris as a character is introduced and immediately establishes himself as an original voice. This is the episode's standout comedy moment.

jokes.text: Daniel, reading 'The Monster Manual' (D&D handbook) alone in school, is approached by Harris the geek who tells him: 'You'd make a good Dungeon Master. I can tell.'
jokes.setup: Daniel is isolated from the freaks and clearly at a low point, reading by himself.
jokes.punchline: Harris's sincere D&D recruitment pitch — evaluating Daniel's Dungeon Master potential on instinct — to the school's coolest (and most troubled) burnout.
jokes.explanation: A lovely character moment with comedic texture. Harris has evaluated Daniel's Dungeon Master potential on sight — presumably based on some quality he perceives in Daniel's brooding — and delivers the assessment with complete authority. The comedy is gentle here; it's also kind of endearing that Harris sees something in Daniel that others miss. Character-specific to both.

jokes.text: Daniel: 'I don't even like thinking about school 'cause I think about how bad I'm really doing. I was even left back once.' / Harris: 'Ouch. Well... Today is the first day of the rest of your life.' / Daniel: 'Someone sent my dad a card with that on it for his 65th birthday.'
jokes.setup: Harris offers Daniel an inspirational platitude after his admission of academic failure.
jokes.punchline: Daniel immediately deflates the platitude by sourcing it — it was on a birthday card for a 65-year-old. The implication: 'first day of the rest of your life' is more appropriate for someone who is mostly finished with life.
jokes.explanation: Elegant structure. Harris's well-meaning platitude is a genuine attempt to help. Daniel's counter-source isn't cynical — it's just accurate and specifically deflating. The 65th birthday context transforms the self-help cliché into something bleak and funny. Then the follow-up ('Your dad's 65?' '70 now. When I'm 45 he'll be 100') extends the joke beautifully.

jokes.text: Harris: 'Your dad's 65?' / Daniel: '70 now.' / Harris: 'When I'm 45, he'll be 100.' [laughs]
jokes.setup: They've established the father is 65 (actually 70).
jokes.punchline: Harris immediately does the future arithmetic — 'when I'm 45 he'll be 100' — and finds this funny. It's not clear if he's laughing at or with Daniel.

jokes.text: Harris, after Daniel compliments him as comfortable in his own skin: 'Yeah, I guess I do. [beat] I don't have sex, though.'
jokes.setup: Daniel has just complimented Harris on having it together. Harris accepts the compliment graciously.
jokes.punchline: Harris volunteers the caveat — returning to his own loser-taxonomy — clarifying that he has inner peace but not the sex that would fully disqualify him from the loser category.

jokes.text: The Mathletes' pre-competition pep talk ends with a barely-audible collective 'yeah!' instead of a roar.
jokes.setup: Kowchevski delivers a genuine pep talk — 'let's go kick some Lincoln butt!' — with legitimate coaching energy.
jokes.punchline: The response is a tiny, unenthusiastic collective 'yeah' — the gap between football-team expectation and mathlete reality.
jokes.explanation: A visual/reaction joke. The set-up is Kowchevski treating this like a sports moment; the punchline is the non-response. Great economy — the gap between the energy of the ask and the energy of the response is the whole joke. Freaks and Geeks does this type of joke beautifully.

jokes.text: Two students mock Sam's jumpsuit outside school: 'Excuse me, aren't you Danny Terrio from Dance Fever?' / 'No, I told you, it's Chrissy from Three's Company.'
jokes.setup: Sam is waiting outside school in his jumpsuit.
jokes.punchline: Two students debate which 1980 TV personality Sam most resembles in his outfit — both reads are devastating.

jokes.text: Alan's bullying of Sam: 'Just when I think he's as queer as he can be, you go and do something even queerer.' — delivered with theatrical bafflement.
jokes.setup: Alan arrives to torment Sam about the jumpsuit.
jokes.punchline: The escalating-queer formulation — 'as queer as he can be,' then 'something even queerer' — suggests Alan has been keeping mental notes on Sam's queerness levels.
jokes.explanation: Alan's bullying is authentic to the era but the humor is mostly cringe/discomfort. The escalating structure is the only comedic craft element. Period-accurate but not especially sophisticated.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso gives Sam a ride home — apparently in character, having become involved in Sam's nightsuit crisis. Sam explains he 'needs a ride home.' This is followed by Rosso's apparent pickup of Sam in his car.
jokes.setup: Alan has been bullying Sam, Rosso intervenes, and somehow the result is Rosso driving Sam home.
jokes.punchline: Rosso's 'intervention' escalates to chauffeuring a student — his instinct for over-involvement in student problems.
jokes.explanation: Rosso as the counselor who over-invests in students is a running character note. This moment is more setup for his subsequent car conversation with Sam than a standalone joke. Functional.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso's story about rednecks making him dance and bark like a dog: 'Did you do it?' / 'Yeah. Pretty much had to. There were 10 of them.'
jokes.setup: Rosso is telling an inspirational story about persevering against mockery — 'I never lost pride in who I am.'
jokes.punchline: The story is supposed to be about dignity and resilience, but it includes him barking like a dog because there were 10 of them. The pragmatic admission — 'pretty much had to' — undercuts the inspiration.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Rosso is a well-meaning guidance counselor telling an inspirational story that accidentally reveals he barked like a dog at redneck insistence. His moral — 'I never lost pride in who I am' — survives somehow, which is the funniest part. 'Pretty much had to, there were 10 of them' is superb — it's honest and deflating in equal measure. The casual acceptance of his own indignity, followed by a pivot to dignity, is perfectly Rosso. Reaches 8+.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso, on confidence: 'If I say I'm the coolest guy in the world, and I believe I'm the coolest guy in the world, then suddenly I become the coolest guy in the world.'
jokes.setup: Rosso is explaining confidence theory to Sam.
jokes.punchline: The meta-joke: Rosso himself is the evidence against this theory. He clearly believes he's cool. He is a school guidance counselor who once barked like a dog.
jokes.explanation: Rosso as a sincere practitioner of his own theory, with himself as unwitting counterexample, is very funny. The speech sounds earnest but lands as unintentional self-parody. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Rosso shows Sam a mirror and says 'Take a look at this kid' / 'Because that's a cool kid.' The mirror is apparently very small or the moment is played straight.
jokes.setup: Rosso wraps up his confidence speech and goes to the physical demonstration.
jokes.punchline: The earnest confidence-mirror moment is played so straight, in the context of Sam wearing the jumpsuit, that it becomes absurd.
jokes.explanation: The earnestness of Rosso doing a confidence mirror on a boy in a Parisian Nightsuit is the joke. It works because the show plays it sincerely rather than winking.

jokes.text: The geeks working out the confidence theory: 'If I think I'm cool, then people will think I'm cool, too.' / 'Yeah, that's what Mr. Rosso said.' / 'Yeah, and he's cool.' / [Beat] 'Yeah, but I already think I'm cool.' / 'Maybe that's — so am I. But nobody — because you're not — I'm really cool.'
jokes.setup: The geeks are applying Rosso's confidence theory.
jokes.punchline: The simultaneous collapse as all three try to assert they already think they're cool, realize this means nothing has been working, and talk over each other.

jokes.text: Bill: 'You know what, Sam? You actually do seem cooler all of a sudden. I think it's working. Mr. Rosso's some kind of genius.'
jokes.setup: Sam has just been through a thoroughly humiliating day in a jumpsuit.
jokes.punchline: Bill's sincere conclusion — 'Mr. Rosso's some kind of genius' — based on Sam seeming fractionally less pathetic, is perfectly earnest.

jokes.text: Millie, as Lindsay is leaving the mathlete party: 'Are you gonna go use your own bathroom?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is making an exit after realizing she can't go back to being a mathlete. She's struggling to explain herself.
jokes.punchline: Millie's pragmatic first interpretation — bathroom — of Lindsay's sudden need to leave is so innocent it's devastating.
jokes.explanation: A great Millie line at a genuinely emotional moment. Lindsay is trying to articulate an identity crisis, and Millie interprets 'I have to go' as a bathroom need. The joke doesn't undercut the emotion — it makes it more poignant. Only Millie asks this. Reaches 8 because of perfect character specificity and the emotional texture it creates.

jokes.text: Millie's final request: 'But can we still play Uno sometimes? I mean, when you've got nothing else to do?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just told Millie she can't be a mathlete anymore.
jokes.punchline: Millie returns to the Uno offer — the one she made at the beginning when Lindsay was in crisis. Now it's the only friendship maintenance she's requesting. 'When you've got nothing else to do' is heartbreaking and funny simultaneously.

jokes.text: Kim suggests a foreign film. Daniel: 'A foreign film? Is that the kind you have to read?' / Kim: 'The point is trying not to be a dumb ass for once in your life, that's what.'
jokes.setup: Kim suggests a midnight foreign film as group activity.
jokes.punchline: Daniel's objection — 'Is that the kind you have to read?' — reveals his relationship with subtitles as labor. Kim's response is also funny in its bluntness.
jokes.explanation: Daniel objecting to subtitles as reading is a character-perfect joke. His priorities are clear. Kim's rebuttal ('trying not to be a dumb ass for once') is unexpectedly self-aware from Kim — she's grown slightly. The specific complaint about 'the kind you have to read' is quotable.

jokes.text: Nick: 'It just better not be about a guy who talks a lot.'
jokes.setup: Nick has agreed to see the foreign film.
jokes.punchline: Nick's one film requirement — no talky guys — is funny both as a movie preference and as character-specific: Nick himself talks a lot.
jokes.explanation: Nick's movie criticism — 'not about a guy who talks a lot' — from the most verbose character in the show is the whole joke. The self-unawareness is perfect. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: Lindsay rejoins the freaks asking to come to the movie. Daniel: 'Can we borrow your dad's car?' / Everyone: 'Awww.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just rejoined the group. Daniel immediately and instinctively asks for the car.
jokes.punchline: Daniel's reflex — car-borrowing — the VERY thing that wrecked the episode, expressed as a welcome-back greeting. The 'awww' from others suggests even they know it was too soon.

jokes.text: Nick finds Lindsay after she rejoins the group: 'Hey, Lindsay? I just wanted to tell you that I'm really glad that you're feeling better about our break-up. I think you're handling it really well.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has rejoined the freaks. Nick approaches with magnanimous generosity.
jokes.punchline: Nick is still operating in his alternate-universe version of events where he broke up with Lindsay and she's recovering. His 'I'm really glad you're feeling better' is patronizing and delusional simultaneously.

jokes.text: Ken: 'Hey, I didn't get all gussied up to sit here alone, man.'
jokes.setup: Nick has stopped to make his speech to Lindsay, leaving Ken alone.
jokes.punchline: 'All gussied up' from Ken Miller — who presumably looks exactly the same as always — is unexpectedly dainty language. His impatience with Nick's emotional processing is the joke.
jokes.explanation: 'Gussied up' is such a period-grandmother expression from Ken Miller that it's immediately funny. His complete intolerance for Nick's emotional moments is consistent character comedy. Good economy.

tmdb_overview: Neal learns his father is cheating on his mother. Ken develops a crush on the tuba player in the school's marching band and Lindsay helps set them up.
